name: LitSearch-style subset (CS/NLP, 12 queries)
source_note: These 12 queries are written in LitSearch (EMNLP 2024) style — natural-language research questions in CS/NLP with citation-graph gold standards. For the OFFICIAL LitSearch v1 dataset (597 queries), download from https://github.com/princeton-nlp/LitSearch (MIT license) and replace this file's `queries` array with the official `queries.jsonl`. The 12 queries below are synthesized for development testing only.
license_note: Original LitSearch is MIT-licensed (Princeton NLP); these synthesized samples follow the same form and may be freely modified.

expected_field_format.query: natural-language research question (one sentence, no quotes)
expected_field_format.gold_papers: list of DOI strings; the papers a domain expert considers indispensable for the query (typically 5-15 papers per LitSearch query)

queries.query: How do tokenization-free language models perform compared to subword-based architectures on multilingual tasks?
What are the methods for evaluating chain-of-thought prompting reliability across reasoning benchmarks?
How does retrieval-augmented generation handle factual consistency in long-form question answering?
What techniques improve sample efficiency in offline reinforcement learning from human feedback?
How do vision-language models handle compositional generalization in visual question answering?
What are the most effective methods for calibrating uncertainty in deep neural network predictions?
How does mechanistic interpretability research identify induction heads in transformer language models?
What are the empirical scaling laws for compute-optimal training of large language models?
How do diffusion models compare to GANs for high-resolution image synthesis quality and diversity?
What approaches enable parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models for downstream tasks?
How do agent-based LLM systems coordinate multi-step tool use and external API calls?
What are the bias and fairness considerations when deploying speech recognition models across demographic groups?
